sentences.text: Yes.
The-- the same day, she was brought back.
And we were together, staying in the bunker.
We were sitting there.
And then two days or three days later, we were taken into the city itself.
We were put into a gymnasium, a tremendous, big school.
That we were put into the basement, to-- under those seats, we were seated.
And the water was like ankle deep.
And we stayed there.
We used to get food once a day.
Whereas when we were in the lager, in the bunker, we got no food for a whole month.
And one day, as we were standing Zahlappell, after Zahlappell I had noticed that there was a big building next to it.
And there was like a-- a basement, those strips of metal that gives the air for the basement.
And I seen one little carrot hanging there that got frozen to-- and as I laid down to get it, the whole metal enclosure, like the gate, just went in.
So I fell into the basement.
When I woke up, there was a pitchfork front of my eyes and the German civilian with a gun standing over me.
And he was saying, [GERMAN].
I was pantomizing that I don't know where.
I mean, I don't know how to go back.
So then he was motioning me to the-- the-- the-- that gate had fallen in, that there it was a metal step ladder built into the wall.
So I-- finally I pulled myself up.
I was broken up.
I pulled myself up from one thing to leave, and I went upstairs.
When I go upstairs, there was the German soldier standing there.
And the minute I stepped up, pulled myself up, and the lot of girls were standing there.
He goes and he takes out the gun from his shoulder, and he's ready to shoot me.
So as broken up I was, I ran into the door, to the basement.
And I just disappeared because there were so many girls over there.
And he was running after me.
I sat down in one of the seat in the basement, and I was sitting there.

When we were standing Zahlappell later on-- because they wanted to see if anybody is missing-- and I felt very bad that I was the cause of it always, the-- the Zahlappell and the-- the nailing and all that.
I mean, kneeling.
So what happened is that they were counting if anybody escaped, if we were trying to escape.
My-- meantime my wrist was puffing up and-- and it was getting bigger.
So I tore off a piece from my dress.
I put some snow in it, ice, and I bundled it up.
And it hurt me for a long time.
Then one day they took us to the train station.
And they took us to another town which we didn't know where we are.
And--
Yes.
Yeah.
We didn't get first class transportation.
We were-- we were happy to go in a cattle car.
We were just hoping they're not going to take us back to Auschwitz.
So we went to this town-- this city.
And we really didn't see the inside of the city because we were marched around, and we were taken off to a barrack that was up on a hill, sort of.
It was fenced around.
Just like we were fenced around in Nuremberg, but it was no electricity in it.
This one had no electricity in it either.
So we were there.
And the next day, they selected a few of us.
And we found out that we are in a place called Holysov, which is a Sudeten place.
And we had seen the German headquarters was like down the hill.
And there were some other barracks.
There were some other French prisoners or half-lings, whichever way you want to call it.
So we were marched to the woods, to the forest.
We went there, and to our surprise there was a few steps to go down and there was a tremendous big ammunition factory.
So they put us to the machine.
And I was supposed to push it when the machine gun was going by to put the lid on it.
I didn't have the strength.
I couldn't do it.
So one of the French men said that you go back, and I will do it.
So I went back.
There was a long sit.
We were filling up machine bullets with phosphate powder.
That powder, like an acetate powder, was so strong.
Was going down to our empty stomach.
We were vomiting left and right.
Then one day-- meantime, the-- the finished machine bullets and the acetate bomb that about this long, and about this big.
We filled it up with gunpowder.
They were fitting-- putting-- packing it into wooden boxes, and there was a chain that was taking it to the railway station.
So it was going that way.
And one day, there was a bombing.
And they bombed the whole line that was carrying the ammunition to the railway station.
The railway station was bombed down.
And my oldest sister was taken there to pick up-- I don't know what to pick up.
And just then she found a few beans that was the rain soaked and all that.
So she picked it up, and we were in seven heaven that now we going to have beans.
We ate that soaked beans.
It almost killed us.
It wasn't for our stomach.

So what happened is that later on when they couldn't manufacture, they couldn't use us to manufacture this machine bullets and all that, they had put us out that we should camouflage the area until they rebuild it.
So they had different colors rocks in the forest.
We had to pick it up, put it in a wheelbarrow, carry it, and put it in a different place so that from the air, they couldn't spot it, that is-- that's the same.
They will come.
Meantime, there was this French prisoner who put a piece of bread under the tree, under the leaves.
And he was motioning to me that which tree, and so on.
I went there.
And for the love of God, I couldn't find the bread.
I was busting.
I was-- I was a-- how-- how can it be?
Finally I found the bread.
The meantime, the French prisoner put up the hand.
He said, thank God, you know, that I found the bread.
And this was going on.
And previous to this, one day as we were marched back from the factory, for the night shift, I see that they took my sister Elizabeth.
And her eyes was all cried out.
And one of this mean [GERMAN] punched her, and she had a big heavy gold ring.
She punched her that her eyes was like-- their skin was almost like split.
And she has-- her eyes is cried out, and she is marching with them.
And I seen that I wanted to change place with her, but I couldn't.
Because those lousy [GERMAN], they were always watching us.
My heart was bleeding.
My heart was going out.
So this was going on, and then the bombing came again.
And then again.
And finally, one day when I was outside at the potato [HUNGARIAN], the bomb-- the planes were going, and they were shooting machine guns.
So the [GERMAN] had all scattered, and all the girls had scattered.
But I didn't want to scatter.
I want to be hanging around the potato [HUNGARIAN].
So I went a little further.
And there was this gutter with the cement [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thing in a-- where the water flows through.
So as the planes were flying by, I had seen that one of the pilot just pull the handle, sort of, and the machine gun was fire-- and bullets were coming out.
So I didn't know what to do, to put my head into the cement thing, or put my behind in it.
So I said if the bullet hits me, maybe it's better if it hits my behind, in my leg, than my head.
And the meantime, some of the girls from the barrack had seen me laying down.
So I heard the way they kept on hollering, Czigler, your sister is dead.
She's laying in the gutter.
She is dead.
Then I hollered back, I'm not dead, so don't tell her I'm dead.
I don't know where I get the nerve.
I really don't mean it.
Then one day as we were again at the potato [HUNGARIAN], I threw the potatoes, when the [GERMAN] wasn't watching, through the fence.
And it was a very big fence.
And my sister would catch it, Clara.
Then some of the potato that would smash on the cement, the other girls licked it up, and they had a picnic out of it.
But if any of the German [GERMAN] had noticed that I lift up my arm and I'm throwing the potatoes, I would get such a beating I would be right there.
One day, there were this girl who I befriended.
Because to tell you the honest truth, I figured it out that eventually we going to be liberated.
So we didn't know if it's going to be the Russians or the English or who.
So I was trying to be selfish, and I was trying to befriend girls who spoke different languages.
So I figured, this way I will able to communicate to-- to-- to be alive.
I felt so stupid that I didn't know German.
I-- I-- I really-- maybe that was my luck.
So what happened is that I befriended this girl.
She was also from around the upper part of the mountains.
And she was a beautiful person.
A very, very lovely looking, very nice.
She was a rabbi's daughter.
One morning, she came to me.
She said, Magda.
I dreamt with my father, which I never did before.
And I was complaining and I was crying that we can't take this anymore.
It's too much.
So my father puts his hand on my head and says, my dear daughter-- you know, in Yiddish-- be patient.
When you eat an apple, you start at the skin, and then you get to the core.
So you will get there.
This was in the morning that she told me this.
They were taking them out to the forest somewhere.
To do what, don't ask me.
I don't know.
They brought her back dead.
They were bombing, and the way the bomb came, from the pressure lifted her up and throw her down, and she was dead.
So the Jewish custom that when somebody dies, you have to sit with them because you don't leave a dead body alone.
Meantime, some of the girls who took down the map from the Nuremberg gymnasium, they peeled it off with their little nails, and they made-- they find beautiful linen.
Very strong linen.
So some of them was nice enough, they shared it.
They gave me some.
And we were sewing it up as a shroud for her.
I stayed with her all night long.
Nobody wanted to stay with the dead body.
So I said, she is my friend.
I have to do it.
Next day, one of the [GERMAN] brought in a-- a-- a-- like an ax.
And I was chopping the earth, and I was trying to make a hole for her.
So I managed.
It was very tiring.
I was very weak, but I did it.
My sister Clara helped me.
Elizabeth helped me.
Elise ?] somehow, she never got involved.
She never bothered herself.
So we buried her into that ground.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next-- two days later, we see that the [GERMAN] doesn't come to open the door, and everything is in an uproar.
We hear a going, coming.
We seen some-- through the hole of the barrack, we see some civilian who used to be [GERMAN].
They were wearing their private civilian clothes.
And they are coming and going towards the forest, and coming back and so on.
So we said that something is not right.
So what happened is that the gate opens, and here marches the German head of the whole group.
And he counts us down.
And one of the head of the civilian who comes.
Which that time, we didn't know who they are.
And he came.
He counted them down.
And he shook hand, and he gave us-- handed over us.
We found--
We found out that these people who liberated us, they were the Yugoslav partisans.
And they weren't supposed to come in to liberate us, because they didn't have backups.
But the Czechoslovak people, the partisans itself, they were informing them that all night long the night before, they were carrying all the ammunition up on the roof.
And they want to blow us up because they don't want no trace of the half-lings.
And when the Yugoslav got wind of it, that they want to explode the whole barrack the next night, that same day they marched in without backups.
And some of the civilians promised that they will help if it's anything.
And they knew exactly how many German soldiers there, how many German woman there, and everything.
And when they came, they lined up all the Germans in their quarters.
And I seen it where we were standing, that one of the German was talking, saying something.
So one of the partisan just took the gun, shot him right in his stomach.
I was so happy.
But so happy to see that.
So then after that, all the partisans, they came up.
And they made a-- a ring around us.
And they put their gun with the bayonet to the ground.
They put their arm on it, and they were crying.
So I looked at my sister.
I said, Clara, we must look disgusting.
If this hardened up man who goes through the woods and everything, and they look at us and they start to cry, then we must be a pathetic-- pathetic sight.
Was Elizabeth with you also?
Yeah.
We were all four of us.

So what happened is after that, they had asked-- the partisans had asked us that being that it was May the 5th, that we should-- whoever feel strong enough to carry a gun, that we should go with them because many of the German soldiers are hiding in the forest, that we should able to shoot them.
So naturally, I wasn't in that shape, and I wouldn't go.
Meantime, I was looking for this woman who stole the blanket from us in-- in Nuremberg.
And she went with them, because I told her that time.
I said, you are such a bastard.
As a Jewish mother, you have a child, and you made us get this beating.
I said, after the war, I will kill you.
And I said, I'm not just saying it.
I will kill you.
Do you know, she was the first one to go with the partisans, and she disappeared.
I never seen her.
And the Stubenalteste and all the big horses who were stealing us blind with the bread and all that, they all went with the partisans.
So we were left without any supervision.
We--
Well, I would say we were left about 200, 220, something like that.
So--
We stayed there about a month, until we started to get to our feet.
But the thing is that when they liberated us, this Yugoslavs, some of the sick girls who were in one of this little room, one of them I befriended already in Nuremberg.
She kept on calling me and calling me.
So I go in because actually she wanted to give me her bread, that I should give her her soup-- my soup, because she couldn't eat the bread.
Her teeth was very loose.
So I told her, no.
I can't do that.
Because if you die, it's going to be my conscience.
Why should I take your bread away?
So she was calling me and calling me.
She said, Czigler, take me out.
I want to see the sunshine.
I want to see the-- the world.
So I said, OK.
So I got one of this four wheeled flat carriage, and I put her on.
Then the rest of them started to scream.
Czigler, if you took her out, take me out.
So I kept on taking them.
And I was so weak, but I kept on taking them.
And after I took them out, I figured, let me take them to the German headquarters, and there they will have better accommodation than this full of lice place.
So I took them down.
And as I was taking down, it was like in a hill going down.
I almost killed myself because the-- the way the wheel.
And my sisters were helping.
All my sisters were helping.
We get down there, and I put each one of them into one of the Germans people bed.
And I said, whenever I see somebody, I will tell them to send an ambulance or send something to take you out.
Meantime, while I was busy taking these girls and arranging it, all the other half-lings, including the French, they all went into the storage place of the Germans.
They packed themselves up with a lot of goodies, lot of canned stuff, lot of materials.
And I had nothing.
So I missed upon it.
So we made a-- a little thing like my brother did in the ghetto, in Kisvarda.
And we were cooking the potatoes.
Meantime, the Americans came in.
And they said that we are here to liberate you.
Now you are free.
You could go wherever you want.
If you want to eat, you could come to our kitchen.
We have a kitchen.
You have to be there in the morning and at night, and you will get meal.
But it was so far away into town that we didn't have the strength to walk.
And by the time we would walk there, those American soldiers were cursing us because they were in the middle of preparing lunch already when we arrived for breakfast.
Because we just didn't have the strength to walk fast.
So once I remember, I get like a pancake or the waffle.
Was sweet like anything.
Were turning my stomach.
As much as I enjoyed it, it was too rich.
But too rich.
So then we didn't even bother to go anymore.
We just cooked our potatoes.
But the meantime, the American soldiers were coming around with the blanket under their arm, and Fraulein [GERMAN], [GERMAN], [GERMAN] so I sent them in a very nice place in my own language.
And I ask him, cigarette?
Oh, right away he took out the cigarette package.
And he had such a fine fingers that three, four cigarette at once that by the time the four of us [BACKGROUND NOISE] went through the package, it was hardly any left over.
Because we had heard that there is a place in Pilsen there a baker will give you bread for the cigarette.
So we collected the cigarette this way.
And I started walking to Pilsen.
Almost a whole day.
When I get there, the bakery I found, I didn't speak German.
And I gave him the cigarette.
He gave me the bread.
I went back.
I took-- said to Clara, never again.
I can't do it.
I almost slept on the road.
I almost fainted.
I'm not strong enough to do that.
So the days were passing by.
Then we had heard that-- oh, yes.
And then the rabbi came in, and he made a lecture that we should be all good little girls the way our mother had taught us because they are watching us.
And we should make sure that we will take care of ourselves, and all that.
Meantime, while she-- while he was preaching, I snuck out.
I went to his Jeep.
I took out the glove compartment, and I took out his chocolate and I took out his nylon stocking.
I figured, you preaching water.
In the meantime, you drink wine.
So you won't drink the wine.
I will have the chocolate.
And then he gave a prayer book to each group.
Like if it was four sister, he gave one.
And then he gave-- so on.
And he-- which I'm surprised.
He didn't sign his name, but he signed the person name whom he gave it to.
Doesn't make much sense because if I show you the prayer book today, you would say, but his-- his name is not in it.
So anyway, we didn't have a pen.
So I can assure you, I didn't write the name in it.
So what happened is after we found that there was a burgomeister in town, we went there, and he gave us a certificate where we were able to travel free of charge.
So we boarded the train.
And we wanted to go home, because we figured that my brother most likely will be alive.
And we want him to know that we are.
So as we were going-- Back to Mandok?
Back to Mandok.
It wasn't too bad while we were going through Czechoslovakia, because the Americans occupied that part.
But when we got to, like Praga, then the problem begin because there was all Russian soldiers.
And we didn't have where to sleep, where to go.
So there was a-- a man who said, there is a big school over there.
And you could go there, and you could sleep there.
By the time, we were-- meantime I hurt myself.
So anyway, I was limping.
By the time we get there, the door was closed.
They said that we filled it, no more room for you.
So we went back and we were sitting opposite the station up on the hill between the few trees.
Next morning, the girls came out.
They say, each one of us was raped.
This Russian soldier went in.
They knew already.
They had a method.
They know that all these little innocent girls are coming back.
And this-- whoever it was, he shouldn't be here.
He was the one who was encouraging the girls to come, and you going to stay.
Well, I'm sure that he made money on it.
That he got a lot of goodies.
And he asked the girls to go there to sleep, and then he let the Russian soldiers in, and he made business with it.
And I said, thank God that we didn't go there.
That he didn't have room for us.
We were seated around the park, and there were bushes around us.
And as we were sitting there, I feel that somebody is trying to grab me, you know?
So I look up.
I see a hairy hand.
I jumped up.
I said to my sister, let's get the hell out of here, because they are trying to catch us.
So we ran away.
I don't know if they caught any of the other girls, but we ran away.
We ran up to the hill where we were the night before.
And I said to myself that this is impossible.
I mean, you don't have peace.
You don't have to be afraid of the gas chamber, but now they want your body.
So finally there was a train that was going through [POLISH] from Praga.
So there were full of Russian soldiers.
So we were standing outside on the step.
I couldn't sit down.
I-- I don't know how I did it.
If I take the wrong move, the wheel would take off my leg for sure.
So finally, we get to [POLISH].
In [POLISH], as we are sitting at the station, my sister was exhausted.
We were exhausted.
And we were sitting there.
I was standing.
She was sitting.
There comes a Russian soldier, and he opens his flies, and he throws my sister down.
And he's ready to lay on her.
I got so angry.
I don't know where I got my nerve.
I pushed him off.
And I said to my sister, you stupid.
Get up.
And jump up.
What-- what are you sitting there for?
What are you waiting for?
And the soldier, as he was trying to get up, I gave him a slap.
That he-- he just shook up.
I started hollering.
I said to the people there, who were at this railway station.
I said, why don't you people come and help?
Don't you see that he wants to rape my sister?
You people, what's the matter with you?
You don't hear, you don't see?
So one of the woman says that now you getting what we were getting during the war.
So now you know who are the Russians.
I said to her, you couldn't come and help us?
I mean, why do-- why do you have to stay there for?
Come and help.
So what happened is that when-- after I gave him the slap, he jumped up.
And he takes up the gun.
And I'm standing there.
I'm going nearer and nearer.
And I said to him, go ahead, shoot me, you so and so and so.
I said, I went through Auschwitz.
We went through Hitler's persecution, and all that.
And now you want to rape us?
Now you want to persecute?
I was hollering, but hollering.
So he put back the gun.
And I thought that he going to give me a slap, that my head going to fell off.
He extended his hand to shake hand.
After he left, my leg just went underneath me, and I fainted.
Because usually I'm that way.
I'm very good at the crisis.
But after that, I just collapse.
So this was one of the incident.
Well, when we went back to Mandok, first we were in Budapest and they were sterilizing us, that we had to go through a bath and so and so.
We arrived there.
They said, you're not going to get any meal unless you get the ticket.
You get the ticket if you are sterilized.
The place was closed.
We couldn't be sterilized.
So I go to the woman at the office.
I said to her, give me the ticket so I could get the food.
She said, if you are not sterilized-- you didn't bring me the ticket from the sterilize, you not going to get the meal.
Meantime, the phone rang.
So I went and I put my hand underneath the ticket, and I took a handful.
So when she turned around, with a big-- big shot, that now you can.
She had no compassion whatsoever.
That--
That these people are hungry, that they want to eat.
So I went and I stayed in line.
I had the ticket because I stole it while she was busy.
In-- in few seconds.
So when she passes by, she said, you're not supposed to stay in here.
You're not going to get food.
I said, oh, yes I do.
She said, where did you get the ticket?
I said, here, I have it.
What's the different where I get it for?
She said, but I want you to tell me where I get the ticket.
I said, big shot.
You want to know where I get the ticket?
I went through Auschwitz, OK.
Which you didn't.
So while you talked on the phone in that split second when you turned your back, I stole the ticket from you.
Now you know where I get the ticket.
I said, you are the meanest person.
You have no compassion whatsoever.
We have to break for tape change.
